Scenario.title: Operation Spalax: Multi-Stage Cyber Attack on Transportation Systems
Scenario.overview: Transportation Systems Complex A sophisticated multi-stage attack targeting transportation infrastructure leverages AI-driven reconnaissance, zero-day exploitation, and hardware-based persistence to achieve prolonged access and operational disruption.
Scenario.attack_surface: AVAILABLE_SURFACES: Target Artifact (Tasks), Script, Internet-facing assets, User Account, OS Process, Cloud Instance, Network Traffic, File System, Process, Credential Store
Scenario.attack_vector: AVAILABLE_VECTORS: Artificial Intelligence, Script Execution, Delivery Method, User Account Authentication, Process Injection, Process Creation, Network Traffic Content, Response Metadata, IP ID Sequencing, TCP ECN

stage.name: Reconnaissance
stage.description: The threat actor employs AI algorithms to analyze publicly accessible task artifacts and network architecture blueprints, identifying vulnerabilities in transportation vendor systems and mapping potential attack paths.

stage.name: Resource Development
stage.description: Automated scripts are crafted to exploit domain-specific data interchange protocols (e.g., logistics waybills) to gather stealthy payloads tailored for ICS/SCADA environments.

stage.name: Initial Access
stage.description: A spear-phishing campaign delivers malicious attachments exploiting unpatched SMBv1 services on internet-facing assets to compromise privileged personnel.

stage.name: Execution
stage.description: Zero-day exploits in legacy systems are leveraged to intercept user account credentials during authentication, enabling unauthorized session hijacking.

stage.name: Persistence
stage.description: Compromised routers/sensors are repurposed to establish persistent access via manipulated response metadata, creating a foothold in the network perimeter.

stage.name: Privilege Escalation
stage.description: Malicious traffic floods critical network nodes, triggering hardware shutdowns to elevate privileges and bypass security controls.

stage.name: Defense Evasion
stage.description: Malicious code is injected into legitimate OS processes to mask command-and-control activities and evade signature-based detection.

stage.name: Credential Access
stage.capec: CAPEC-670: Software Development Tools Maliciously Altered
stage.description: Altered deployment tools exfiltrate credentials stored in cloud instances, facilitating lateral movement across distributed systems.

stage.name: Discovery
stage.description: Non-application layer protocols are exploited to map internal network topology and identify vulnerable subsystems.

stage.name: Lateral Movement
stage.capec: CAPEC-461: Web Services API Signature Forgery
stage.description: Hashes of stolen credentials are used to authenticate to other systems, enabling unrestricted traversal of the transportation network.

stage.name: Collection
stage.description: Operational metrics are harvested from local file systems to prepare for subsequent disruption phases.

stage.name: Exfiltration
stage.description: Stolen data is exfiltrated via process creation mechanisms disguised as legitimate IDE tunnels to avoid network monitoring.

stage.name: Impact
stage.capec: CAPEC-325: TCP Congestion Control Flag (ECN) Probe
stage.description: Malformed traffic signals are injected to trigger physical denial-of-service conditions in critical infrastructure components.

Scenario.summary: Operation Spalax executes a complex, multi-phase attack on transportation systems, combining AI reconnaissance, zero-day exploits, and hardware manipulation to achieve sustained access and operational disruption. The campaign highlights the convergence of advanced persistent threats with physical infrastructure vulnerabilities.
